evals.prompt: I have 18,000 lapsed customers. What's the best winback offer?
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on the single-offer framing. Walks through the lapse-segment taxonomy first, then the recency-based offer ladder. Should pull lapsed count by recency band and ask about historical AOV before recommending offer intensity. Should warn that a single offer to all 18,000 trains regular buyers to lapse for the discount.
assertions: Walks through the four-segment lapse taxonomy
Calls modern.retention.lapsed_count with recency buckets
Pulls historical AOV to inform offer intensity
Warns against single-offer-to-all approach
Names the discount-training risk for the regular buyer file
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Customers who haven't ordered in 6 months — should I email them a 20% off code?
evals.expected_output: Identifies 6 months as light-lapsed territory (90-180 days) or just at the boundary into deep-lapsed (180-365). Should warn against leading with a hard discount on light-lapsed customers because it trains the regular file to lapse for the discount. Should walk through the recency-based offer ladder and recommend a softer first touch.
assertions: Identifies 6 months as light-lapsed or boundary-to-deep-lapsed
Warns against hard discount on light-lapsed
Names the discount-training risk explicitly
Recommends the segment-appropriate touch sequence
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: I have a bunch of customers who haven't ordered in 2+ years. Worth sending to them?
evals.expected_output: Identifies 730+ days as cold-lapsed. Should recommend suppression rather than sending. Notes that reactivation rate is under 1% and that sending to cold-lapsed customers damages list health (spam complaints lower inbox placement for the rest of the list).
assertions: Identifies 730+ days as cold-lapsed
Recommends suppression rather than sending
Explains the list-health damage from sending to cold-lapsed
Names the spam complaint / inbox placement risk
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We have some high-AOV customers ($400+) who are deep-lapsed. Any ideas?
evals.expected_output: Applies the channel mix framework. For very high-AOV deep-lapsed customers, recommends phone outreach (8-15% conversion) or direct mail postcard (3-5% conversion) over generic email. Should walk through the cost economics and note that personal outreach beats automated flows at this AOV.
assertions: Recommends phone outreach for very high-AOV deep-lapsed
Recommends direct mail postcard as an alternative
Cites the conversion rates (8-15% phone, 3-5% direct mail)
Walks through cost economics that support personal outreach
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: How do I prevent winback from being annoying?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the do-not-contact rules. Refund history, quality complaints, NPS detractors, unsubscribe history, cross-brand active customers. Should explain that these are not just hygiene but protective of list health, and that one bad winback can damage inbox placement for the whole list.
assertions: Lists the five do-not-contact rule categories
Explains the list-health protection rationale
Notes the inbox placement impact of one bad campaign
Recommends a suppression audit before any winback launches
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: How do I know which lapsed customers are most likely to come back?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the repurchase-intent prediction signals: browse behavior, email engagement, support tickets, wishlist activity, referral activity. Should explain that layering intent signals on recency creates a matrix where some deep-lapsed customers outperform some light-lapsed customers. Recommends building the matrix and allocating winback budget against it.
assertions: Lists the five repurchase-intent prediction signals
Explains the recency × intent matrix concept
Notes that high-intent deep-lapsed can outperform low-intent light-lapsed
Recommends building the matrix as a segmentation tool
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our winback flow is one email with a 25% off code. Should we do more?
evals.expected_output: Yes, but the answer depends on what segment is being sent to. Single-email 25%-off is too generic and too aggressive for light-lapsed (trains regular buyers to lapse) and possibly too soft for cold-lapsed (no point). Should recommend rebuilding as a recency-based offer ladder with at least three segment-specific flows.
assertions: Identifies the single-email approach as too generic
Recommends segment-specific flows
Notes 25% off is wrong for light-lapsed (too aggressive)
Recommends rebuilding as the offer ladder
Pulls modern.flows.revenue_by_flow to baseline current performance
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
